title: Glucose Monitoring & Ramadan
content: For Algerian patients during Ramadan, fasting blood sugar should be monitored closely. If glucose drops below 70 mg/dL or rises above 300 mg/dL, the fast MUST be broken (Ftar). High risk for dehydration and Ketoacidosis in our hot climate. Recommendation: Consult with a doctor 2 months before Ramadan to adjust insulin/medication doses.
source: Algerian Society of Endocrinology (SAE) / International Diabetes Federation

title: La Tention & Stress
content: In Algeria, stress (Z'aaf) is a major contributor to sudden BP spikes. A BP reading of >= 140/90 mmHg on two separate occasions confirms Hypertension. Urgent care is needed if BP > 180/120 or if there is sudden blurred vision (Mouch f l'ayoun) or chest pain.

title: Metformin & Gastrointestinal Comfort
content: Many Algerians stop Metformin due to 'Dawkha f el kerch' (nausea) or diarrhea. To solve this: Start with a low dose (500mg) and increase gradually. MUST be taken in the middle of a meal (Couscous, Tagine, etc.) to minimize stomach irritation. Never stop without consulting your doctor.

title: The Algerian Plate - Diabetes Friendly
content: Standard Algerian diet is too high in 'Khobz Eddar' (white bread) and sweet mint tea (Atay b sokor). Solution: Replace white bread with whole-grain or barley bread (Chir). Reduce sugar in tea gradually. Increase 'Slata' (salad) intake before the main course to slow sugar absorption.

title: Hidden Salt (El Melh) in Local Food
content: Avoid 'Khlii' (preserved meat), pickles (Torshi), and high-sodium tomato paste commonly used in Algerian stews. Daily salt should not exceed one teaspoon (5g). Use herbs like 'Kousbor' (Coriander) and 'Maadnous' (Parsley) for flavor instead of salt.
source: National Program for Hypertension Control - Algeria

title: Foot Care (R'jline)
content: Diabetes patients in Algeria must check their feet daily for cuts or redness, especially if they walk to the Mosque or market often. Use comfortable shoes. If a wound doesn't heal, seek medical help immediately to avoid 'Djih' (infection/gangrene).
